summary: Fowler addresses whether large projects can use agile techniques, acknowledging that agile methods were designed for smaller teams and haven't been fully proven at scale. He argues that rather than focusing on scaling agile up, teams should first try to scale their projects down. Drawing on experience from XP, FDD, and RUP, he notes that most projects could lose half their people without slowing down, as large teams carry significant communication and management overhead. The counterintuitive conclusion is that smaller teams of more capable people are faster and cheaper, even at higher individual cost.
key_points: Agile techniques haven't been fully proven at large scale yet - new methods are naturally tried on smaller projects first
Different agile approaches have different sweet spots: XP works for up to 20, FDD for mid-tens, RUP has been used with 200+
Most projects could lose about half their people without slowing down
Large teams carry significant overhead in communication and management
Scaling agile up should be the last resort, not the first choice
money_quotes: Scaling Agile methods is the last thing you should do.
A better approach is to try to scale down your project.
Most projects could lose about half the people of the project without making things go slower.
Using smaller teams staffed with more able people is usually faster and cheaper, even if everyone is more individually expensive.
For software projects I think the primary measure of large is number of people.
With any technique or technology I wouldn't recommend using it on anything larger than twice the size of something you've used it successfully already.
key_insight: Instead of asking how to scale agile up to large teams, first ask whether you can scale your project down to fit agile's natural strengths.
